source.title: Predictions not commands: active inference in the motor system

confidence_rationale: Adams, Shipp and Friston (2013) develop the active inference motor framework in which the motor cortex issues descending proprioceptive predictions rather than direct motor commands. The key dual-stream structure is: a top-down prediction stream (what the world should look like given intended action, analogous to pre-commit simulation) and an ascending prediction-error stream that carries realized sensory mismatch back up (analogous to post-commit realized error). This maps onto MECH-060's dual-channel architecture: the generative model's forward pass corresponds to the pre-commit simulation channel, while the ascending prediction error is the post-commit realized-error channel. The precision-weighting mechanism (which modulates how much weight the motor system places on predicted vs. realized sensory feedback) is a close functional analogue to write-boundary enforcement: high motor-level precision attenuates the ascending error signal -- in effect, the pre-commit channel dominates during committed action sequences and post-commit feedback is down-weighted until completion. Confidence at 0.68 because the framing is motor/sensory rather than ethical harm attribution, and the paper does not address contamination consequences when the two channels are conflated.
